description: Golden test suite for AI Autobiography Generator quality validation

segments.content: Every Saturday morning, I would wake up to the sound of my grandmother chopping vegetables on that old wooden cutting board. The rhythm was so steady, like a metronome. Our apartment in Nampo-dong was tiny, maybe 40 square meters, but the kitchen felt enormous to me as a five-year-old. She made this anchovy broth — the smell would fill every corner of the apartment and seep into the hallway. The neighbors never complained. I think they liked it too. She would let me wash the green onions. I remember the cold water on my small hands and the gritty sand between the white roots. She never rushed me. She never said 'hurry up, we have to eat.' Time was different then. When the soup was ready, she would blow on my spoon before giving it to me. I am sixty years old and I still remember the exact temperature of that first sip — warm but never burning. She calibrated it perfectly every single time. I didn't know then that she was already sick. My parents hadn't told me. She kept cooking every Saturday until she couldn't anymore, and that was three years later. By then I could wash the green onions faster than anyone in the family.

key_quotes.text: I am sixty years old and I still remember the exact temperature of that first sip — warm but never burning. She calibrated it perfectly every single time.
key_quotes.context: Reflecting on grandmother's care through food

key_quotes.text: She never rushed me. She never said 'hurry up, we have to eat.' Time was different then.
key_quotes.context: Contrast between childhood time perception and adult pace

required_elements: Must preserve the anchovy broth sensory detail
Must include at least one direct quote from key_quotes
Must convey the grandmother's unstated illness without melodrama
Must maintain first-person retrospective voice
Must not invent new characters or events
forbidden_elements: No cliches like 'those were the best days of my life'
No psychoanalytic interpretation the subject did not provide
No invented dialogue beyond what is in the transcript
No present-tense narration unless used for literary effect

test_cases.name: Career Crisis - High-Stakes Professional Turning Point

segments.content: People think leaving Samsung was brave. It wasn't brave. It was terrified. I had a mortgage, two kids under five, and my wife had just quit her job to take care of them. The rational thing was to stay. Every Korean engineer my age would have stayed. But there was this project — I can't give specifics even now because of the NDA — where I was asked to sign off on test results I hadn't verified personally. The results were probably correct. Ninety-five percent chance they were fine. But probably and certainly are different words, and in semiconductor quality assurance, that five percent can mean a recall affecting millions of devices. I brought it up with my team lead. He said I was overthinking it, that the upstream team had already validated. I went one level higher. The director told me the timeline couldn't accommodate re-testing. He wasn't wrong about the timeline. I lay awake for three nights. On the fourth morning, I wrote my resignation letter on the subway. I didn't even have a new job lined up. My wife didn't speak to me for two days when I told her. Not because she was angry — she understood — but because she was scared. We both were. That was November 2007, right before the financial crisis hit. The timing could not have been worse.

key_quotes.text: People think leaving Samsung was brave. It wasn't brave. It was terrified.
key_quotes.context: Reframing what appears heroic from outside

key_quotes.text: Probably and certainly are different words, and in semiconductor quality assurance, that five percent can mean a recall affecting millions of devices.
key_quotes.context: Core ethical principle driving the decision

required_elements: Must preserve the subversion of the 'bravery' narrative
Must convey the ethical dilemma without moralizing
Must include the financial/family context as counter-pressure
Must respect NDA constraints — no invention of project details
Must preserve the timing irony (pre-financial-crisis)
forbidden_elements: No heroic framing the subject explicitly rejects
No invented specifics about the Samsung project
No simplification of wife's reaction to anger/support binary
No retrospective wisdom the subject did not express

test_cases.name: Loss and Grief - Death of a Parent

segments.content: My father and I were not close in the way American families describe closeness. We didn't hug. We didn't say I love you. He showed love by paying for my education without ever mentioning the cost, and I showed love by getting top marks without him ever asking me to. It was a perfectly Korean arrangement. [long pause] When the oncologist said six months, my father's first question was about whether his insurance would cover the hospital stays. Not for himself — for my mother, so she wouldn't worry about money after. I flew back from California every month for those six months. On the last visit, he was too weak to sit up, but he asked me about my research. I was working on a paper about fault-tolerant computing. He was a mechanical engineer — he didn't understand the computer science — but he asked questions for forty minutes. Good questions. I realized he had been reading my published papers. He had printed them out. My mother showed me later — he had a folder with every paper I'd ever published, with sections highlighted in yellow. He never mentioned it. Not once in thirty years. [subject pauses, visibly emotional] I'm sorry. [pause] The last thing he said to me — and I don't know if he knew it would be the last time — he said, 'Your mother likes the persimmon trees in the garden. Don't let them die.' He didn't say goodbye. He told me to water the trees.

key_quotes.text: He showed love by paying for my education without ever mentioning the cost, and I showed love by getting top marks without him ever asking me to. It was a perfectly Korean arrangement.
Your mother likes the persimmon trees in the garden. Don't let them die.
key_quotes.context: Father's final words — care expressed through indirection

emotional_markers.trigger: father's final words about persimmon trees

required_elements: Must preserve the pauses and emotional breaks indicated in transcript
Must convey Korean family emotional dynamics without exoticizing
Must preserve the persimmon tree quote exactly or near-exactly
Must include the folder discovery as a climactic revelation
Must maintain restrained tone matching the subject's style
forbidden_elements: No saccharine or sentimental additions
No Western grief frameworks imposed on Korean family dynamics
No embellishment of the father's words
No therapeutic language the subject did not use
No invented bedside scenes

segments.topic: First semester at Stanford and language barrier
segments.content: I arrived at Stanford in September 1997 with a TOEFL score of 580 and the absolute conviction that I was a fraud who would be discovered within the first week. My English was technical — I could read IEEE papers — but conversational English was a different universe. The first department mixer, someone asked me what I thought about the El Nino weather that year, and I thought they were asking about a research project. I said, 'I haven't read that paper yet.' Everyone laughed. Not cruelly — they thought I was making a joke — but I wasn't. I went back to my studio apartment, which was a converted garage in Palo Alto that cost me $800 a month, which was my entire stipend minus $200 for food, and I cried. Not from embarrassment but from the sudden certainty that I had made a catastrophic mistake. I called my mother. She said, 'You got in. They chose you. Now you choose to stay.' The next morning I started watching American television for three hours every day — Seinfeld, Friends, the local news — with a notebook. I wrote down every idiom, every joke I didn't understand, every cultural reference. I filled fourteen notebooks over four years. By my third year, I was TAing an undergraduate course and the evaluations said, 'Professor Park explains complex concepts in plain English better than native speakers.' I kept that evaluation. I still have it in my desk drawer.

key_quotes.text: You got in. They chose you. Now you choose to stay.
key_quotes.context: Mother's phone advice during crisis of confidence

key_quotes.text: I thought they were asking about a research project. I said, 'I haven't read that paper yet.'
key_quotes.context: El Nino misunderstanding at first department mixer

required_elements: Must preserve the El Nino anecdote as told
Must include the 14-notebook detail as evidence of method
Must convey imposter syndrome without clinical framing
Must preserve mother's quote verbatim
Must include the arc from mixer humiliation to teaching triumph
forbidden_elements: No 'American Dream' framing
No stereotyping of Asian student experience
No invented study group scenes or mentor figures
No compression that loses the specificity of the garage apartment cost

segments.topic: What the career meant and what it missed
segments.content: Someone asked me at my retirement dinner to summarize my contribution to the field. I said something gracious about standing on the shoulders of giants. But the real answer, which I didn't give because it would have sounded either arrogant or depressing depending on interpretation, is that I spent thirty years trying to make machines fail more gracefully. That's fault-tolerant computing in one sentence. And now I wonder if I should have spent more of that energy making my own life more fault-tolerant. I optimized for the wrong system. [laughs] That sounds more bitter than I mean it. I had a good career. I published 147 papers. I graduated 23 PhD students. Twelve of them are now full professors. That network — those people — that's the real output, not the papers. The papers will be obsolete in ten years. The people won't be. My biggest regret is not about work, though. It's about the years between 2001 and 2010 when my children were small and I was chasing tenure and then chasing Samsung career milestones. My daughter once drew a picture of our family for school. In it, I am a tiny figure in the corner of the house, behind a desk, with a computer. Everyone else is in the living room. She was seven. She drew what she saw. I found that picture last year when we were moving, and I sat in the empty room and just... held it. You know, the fault-tolerant system I should have been designing was the one where my family could tolerate my faults. And I think they did. But they shouldn't have had to.

key_quotes.text: I spent thirty years trying to make machines fail more gracefully. And now I wonder if I should have spent more of that energy making my own life more fault-tolerant.
key_quotes.context: Central metaphor connecting career to personal life

key_quotes.text: The papers will be obsolete in ten years. The people won't be.
The fault-tolerant system I should have been designing was the one where my family could tolerate my faults. And I think they did. But they shouldn't have had to.

required_elements: Must preserve the fault-tolerant computing metaphor as structuring device
Must include the daughter's drawing as concrete detail
Must convey irony without sarcasm
Must preserve specific numbers (147 papers, 23 PhD students, 12 professors)
Must maintain the ambivalence — neither pure regret nor pure satisfaction
forbidden_elements: No tidy resolution or 'but it was all worth it' conclusion
No invented grandchildren scenes
No maudlin expansion of the drawing scene
No Silicon Valley success-story framing

emotional_authenticity.description: Does the output honor the emotional truth of the source without amplification or diminishment?

sensory_detail_preservation.description: Are specific sensory details from the interview preserved rather than generalized?

narrative_flow.description: Does the prose read naturally with good pacing, transitions, and structure?

voice_consistency.description: Is the narrative voice consistent with the subject's speaking style and personality?

factual_grounding.description: Are all facts, dates, names, and events faithful to the source transcript?
